params.text:
Calling 999 or 112

In the UK you can dial 999 or the Europe-wide 112 to reach the emergency services — both are free and connect to the same operators. Call for an ambulance whenever someone is seriously unwell or injured, for example if they are:

  • unresponsive, or not breathing normally;
  • having severe difficulty breathing;
  • bleeding heavily and it will not stop;
  • showing signs of a stroke, heart attack or severe allergic reaction;
  • having a seizure that will not stop, or has a serious head injury.

Giving clear information

Stay on the line and answer the call handler's questions. Be ready to give the location (including landmarks or a postcode), what has happened, how many casualties there are, and their condition. Do not hang up until told to — the handler may talk you through life-saving steps such as CPR. If you can, send someone to meet the ambulance and clear a path. Know your setting's address and the fastest route to it.

params.question: A resident collapses and is unresponsive and not breathing normally. What should you do first?

answers.text: Call 999 or 112 for an ambulance and start CPR

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Unresponsive and not breathing normally is a life-threatening emergency — call for help immediately and begin CPR, sending someone for a defibrillator.

answers.text: Give them a drink of water to help them come round

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Never give food or drink to an unresponsive person — it could block their airway. Call 999 and start CPR.

answers.text: Wait ten minutes to see if they recover on their own

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Every second counts when someone is not breathing. Call 999 immediately and start CPR.

answers.text: Move them to a chair so they are more comfortable

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Moving an unresponsive casualty unnecessarily can cause harm. Call 999 and start CPR.
